title: ‘The Matrix Resurrections’ and the Wachowski Movie Rankings
format: Sean, Amanda, and Rob review Lana Wachowski's solo return to the franchise, then rank all eight Wachowski films from worst to best.

segments.heading: ‘The Matrix Resurrections’
segments.note: Lana Wachowski's solo return to the franchise

films.title: The Matrix Resurrections
films.note: Lana directs alone this time; her sister Lily sits it out. Co-written with novelists David Mitchell (Cloud Atlas) and Aleksandar Hemon. Keanu Reeves is again Thomas Anderson, now a video-game designer whose studio wants a sequel to the trilogy-inspired game he made, the meta hook the whole thing runs on. Neil Patrick Harris plays his analyst, later revealed as a new Architect; Yahya Abdul-Mateen II plays a ‘modal’ version of Morpheus, since Laurence Fishburne wasn't asked back; Jonathan Groff plays Smith, reimagined as Anderson's boss. Sean and Rob land around a 3 to 3.5 out of 5; Amanda closer to a 2.8. All three like the writing and the metatextual first hour, which argues with twenty years of red-pill co-option and Warner Bros.'s own demand for a sequel. The digital cinematography, muddy action, and a back half that turns into standard robot-fighting are the sticking points. Consensus MVP: Lambert Wilson's return as the Merovingian.

segments.heading: Reference points
segments.note: movies and comps that came up along the way

films.title: A Glitch in the Matrix
films.note: Rodney Ascher's Sundance documentary on people who believe they're living in a simulation. Sean brings it up as a companion piece on what happens when a film's meaning slips out of its creator's hands.

films.title: The Animatrix
films.note: The anime anthology tying into the Matrix world. Sean recommends starting a rewatch there (the first two segments work as a real prologue), though it's left out of the ranking below along with the rest of the extended universe.

films.title: Ghostbusters: Afterlife
films.note: Rob's unfavorable comp: he prefers how Resurrections handles being ‘a movie about its own existence’ to what this one does with the same problem.

films.title: Ocean's Twelve
films.note: Amanda's vibe comp for a sequel that spends its runtime interrogating the idea of a sequel.

films.title: Shutter Island
films.note: Rob's comp for the disorienting, what's-real opening act, which he says plays closer to this than to the original trilogy.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: The other new-release measuring stick for how much homework a movie now expects. Amanda could follow this multiverse without difficulty but says Resurrections doesn't open itself up the same way.

films.title: Space Jam: A New Legacy
films.note: Amanda's example of Warner Bros. running the same meta-synergy play twice in one year, raised right after Smith's in-movie pitch about the studio demanding a sequel.

films.title: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
films.note: Rob's comp for a franchise entry that turns subversive against its own fanbase.

films.title: Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon
films.note: Cited for choreographer Yuen Woo-ping, brought up when the hosts note that Resurrections gives a cameo to John Wick director Chad Stahelski instead of putting him to work on the fights.

films.title: John Carter
films.note: Amanda's comp for the tiny robot sidekick Bugs, which reminds her of Woola, and the merchandising push she suspects is behind it.

segments.heading: The Wachowski Movie Rankings
segments.note: worst to best on the show, best-first here; the Animatrix, V for Vendetta, and the Netflix series Sense8 are left off since the hosts stick to features the Wachowskis solely directed
segments.subject: the Wachowskis

films.title: The Matrix
films.note: No. 1, unanimous. Amanda's first-boyfriend memory, Sean's five-star ‘masterpiece of the last quarter century’ seen at 16, Rob's first movie he wasn't old enough to see.

films.title: Speed Racer
films.note: No. 2. Rob's surprise case (candy-colored, all-green-screen racing he says is maybe the best any movie has looked) wins Amanda over as tiebreaker against Sean's objection.

films.title: Bound
films.note: No. 3. Sean's case: in the conversation for best neo-noir ever made, carried by Gina Gershon, Jennifer Tilly, and Joe Pantoliano.

films.title: The Matrix Reloaded
films.note: No. 4. The freeway chase is Sean's Jurassic Park T-Rex comp. Both he and Rob say revisiting this was more rewarding than revisiting Resurrections.

films.title: Cloud Atlas
films.note: No. 5. Co-written by novelist David Mitchell, who also has a story credit on Resurrections. Sean didn't connect with it on release but has come around; both flag the racial makeup choices as a real problem. Tom Hanks has named it one of his three favorite filmmaking experiences.

films.title: The Matrix Resurrections
films.note: No. 6. Sean's placement, mostly because a second viewing of Reloaded beat a second viewing of this.

films.title: The Matrix Revolutions
films.note: No. 7. Sean calls its ending his single biggest nitpick in the franchise, hard to explain even with help. Rob notes it does have more leather.

films.title: Jupiter Ascending
films.note: No. 8. Rob rewatched it ‘in a rubbernecking capacity’ and still isn't sure the movie is good, but loves Eddie Redmayne's whisper-screaming space emperor.

films: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness

Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madn.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
